name: He Xiangu
description: He Xiangu, whose name translates to "Immortal Maiden He," is one of the Eight Immortals in the Taoist pantheon, a group of legendary xian (immortals) revered for their power and ability to grant blessings. She is distinguished as the only female member of this esteemed group, making her a particularly significant figure in Chinese folk religion. Her stories vary, but she is often depicted as a virtuous and compassionate woman who achieved immortality through dedicated spiritual practice. Oral traditions and written accounts indicate that she lived during the Tang Dynasty, though details of her birth and early life are shrouded in mystery. He Xiangu is venerated as a patron of homemakers and is believed to possess the power to heal and protect. Her association with longevity and spiritual enlightenment makes her a popular subject in art and literature, embodying the Taoist ideals of balance, harmony, and the pursuit of immortality through self-cultivation.
subtitle: The Lotus Immortal

keyMyths.title: He Xiangu's Enlightenment
keyMyths.description: According to legend, He Xiangu attained immortality after consuming a magical pearl or cloud-mother stone. This event granted her the ability to fly and subsist without earthly food. She then dedicated her life to spiritual pursuits, wandering the mountains and helping those in need. Her commitment to virtue and her connection to the natural world solidified her status as one of the revered Eight Immortals, demonstrating the Taoist principle of achieving enlightenment through harmony with the Dao.
keyMyths.source: Werner, E. T. C. *Myths and Legends of China*. George G. Harrap & Company, 1922.

keyMyths.title: The Dream Revelation
keyMyths.description: Another prominent myth recounts that He Xiangu received a divine revelation in a dream, where she was instructed to consume a special stone that would grant her immortality. Obeying this celestial guidance, she followed the dream's instructions and achieved her transformed state. This story highlights the importance of dreams and omens in Taoist beliefs, as well as the role of divine intervention in the path to spiritual enlightenment.
keyMyths.source: Lagerwey, John, and Lü Pengzhi. *Early Chinese Religion: The Period of Division (221-589 AD)*, Part Two. Brill, 2010.

keyMyths.title: He Xiangu and the Empress Wu
keyMyths.description: Some accounts claim that Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history, summoned He Xiangu to her court. However, He Xiangu, preferring a life of solitude and spiritual practice, refused to serve the empress. This story illustrates He Xiangu's unwavering commitment to her spiritual path and her rejection of worldly power, further emphasizing her virtuous character and dedication to Taoist principles.
keyMyths.source: Little, Stephen, and Shawn Eichman. *Taoism and the Arts of China*. Art Institute of Chicago, 2000.

sources.author: Werner, E.T.C.
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends, providing detailed accounts of He Xiangu's origins and deeds.
